data.title: Polar Power receives order for mobile EV chargers
data.description:
• Polar Power (POLA) on Friday said that it has received an initial purchase order for fifty EVMC30K EV mobile chargers, designed to provide emergency roadside fast-charging for electric vehicles that have depleted their batteries charge before reaching a stationary charging station.
  • The units provide up to 30 kW using CCS and Tesla charging standards and operate independent of the grid using a propane fueled Toyota prime power engine driving a high efficiency Polar DC alternator for a direct generator to EV charging. 
  • Polar Power’s DC generator technology is known for its compact, lightweight, and low cost performance. The EVMC30K Chargers are also available in natural gas and diesel fuel options and deliver a 15 to 30-minute charge, sufficient to reach the nearest charging station.
  • Polar Power’s customer for these 50 units is currently in business selling level 1 and 2 mobile EV chargers.
  • POLA +1.29% premarket to $2.34.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Joby Aviation accuses rival Archer Aviation of corporate espionage - update
data.description:
Updated 11/21 7:40ET with response from Archer Aviation.

Joby Aviation (JOBY) has filed a lawsuit against rival Archer Aviation (ACHR) in a California Superior Court in Santa Cruz, alleging that confidential information taken by a former employee who went to work for Archer (ACHR) was used to disrupt Joby’s exclusive client partnership.

According to court documents, Joby’s (JOBY) then U.S. state and local policy head, George Kivork, had access to confidential and proprietary information regarding Joby’s strategic partnerships, technical information, and sensitive information that would be valuable to a competitor.

Two days prior to announcing his resignation, Joby (JOBY) alleges Kivork exfiltrated a cache of “highly valuable Joby files.” Less than three weeks later, Joby (JOBY) was notified by a strategic partner that they were approached by Archer (ACHR) with detailed knowledge about the confidential terms of that partner’s agreement with Joby (JOBY).

“This is corporate espionage, planned and premeditated,” Joby states in its lawsuit.

In a statement to CNBC, Archer’s (ACHR) chief legal officer called Joby’s (JOBY) allegations “without merit,” and the complaint “does not identify a single specific trade secret, let alone any evidence of misappropriation.”

A hearing is scheduled for March 20, 2026.

Archer Aviation's (ACHR) Chief Legal and Strategy Officer Eric Lentell provided the following statement to Seeking Alpha regarding allegations against the company from Joby Aviation (JOBY): 

"Joby alleges we used their trade secrets to win a 'deal' with a developer but the reality is that Archer has no deal with this developer and Mr. Kivork did not bring any Joby confidential information to Archer. Joby knows these facts and is now improperly attempting to achieve through bad faith litigation what it cannot accomplish through fair competition. Archer remains focused on building the future of advanced aviation in America."

data.title: Euro Tech unit PACT wins $2.1M water treatment contract
data.description:
• Euro Tech (CLWT) on Friday said its majority-owned subsidiary, Yixing PACT Environmental Technology Company, has secured a contract worth around US$2.1 million.
  • The project aims to provide both sewage and potable water treatment solutions at a uranium mining site for a French-Mongolian joint venture energy company in Mongolia.
  • The contract covers design, supply, fabrication and commissioning and is expected to be completed by August 2026.
  • CLWT -1.78% premarket to $1.09.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: NY Fed's Williams sees room for further rate adjustments in the near term
data.description:
While the Federal Reserve's two rate cuts this year have eased monetary policy some, New York Fed President John Williams still sees some room to adjust policy further in the near term, he said on Friday. That implies he's open to another rate cut in December.

He currently views the central bank's policy as "modestly restrictive," he said in a speech at the Bank of Chile.

"My assessment is that the downside risks to employment have increased as the labor market has cooled, while the upside risks to inflation have lessened somewhat," he said in prepared text. "Underlying inflation continues to trend downward, absent any evidence of second-round effects emanating from tariffs."

Williams estimates that U.S. tariffs have increased the inflation rate, which now stands at ~2.75%, by about a half to three-quarters of a percentage point. While he doesn't expect to see second-round tariffs or other spillover effects from inflation, it will take time for the tariffs' full effects to filter into the economy.

"I expect the effects of tariffs on inflation will play out over the rest of this year and the first half of next year. Inflation should thereafter get back on track to 2 percent in 2027," he said.

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Uncertainty? Disconnect? Correction? All of the above are plaguing markets
data.description:
There is a lot of doubt weighing on markets right now. It appears to be a disconnect between positive economic data and what average Americans are experiencing, confusion over the path of the interest rates and makeup at the Fed, and whether the massive amounts of AI spending will generate enough revenue to justify the investment. More importantly, all of these themes have underwritten the stock and risk asset rally over the last two years, but the visibility has become increasingly murky, as well as the path forward.

Case in point: On Thursday, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) surged out of the gate, bolstered by earnings from Nvidia (NVDA) that were thought to justify the AI rally, as well as powering through a long-delayed jobs report. Only hours later, the tech-heavy index gave up gains of more than 2% to close the session down 2.2%, in the biggest intraday reversal since "Liberation Day" panic seen in April. The Cboe Volatility Index, or VIX, also spiked from below 20 to 28, indicating spreading fear among investors.

Things were already shaky, with indices barreling toward a market correction. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) is down by 5.2% since its last all-time closing high several weeks ago, while the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq (COMP:IND) are off by 5.1% and 7.8%, respectively. A full correction might be due as sentiment continues to turn, with a drop of 10% or more from recent peaks seen as necessary to reset overheated valuations and curb speculative excess.

Something bigger? Stock futures are hugging the flatline early Friday in search of direction, but that hasn't stopped warnings and fears from continuing to spread. "Equities are not the only bubble across the economy that is starting to falter," Investing Group Leader Bret Jenson writes in his latest SA article. In it, he discusses "notable cracks" in artificial intelligence, cryptocurrency, housing, and sovereign debt, along with a dozen charts to support the thesis. (67 comments)
